ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for salted-egg-coated fried chicken; mapped to broader chicken food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for salted-egg-battered fried chicken; A0CKN (cooked chicken meat) used as the closest defensible broader anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared chicken dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Salted Egg Chicken Chunky Pops is approximately 150 g (about 6–8 bite-sized pieces), commonly consumed as a main dish or sharing snack at lunch or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: Salted Egg Chicken Chunky Pops — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 55 kcal, protein 10.7 g, fat 0.0 g, carbohydrate 2.36 g per 100 g) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Salted Egg Chicken Chunky Pops (cr_id G00010399). Fat reported as 0.0 g in the HPB record; this is flagged as anomalous for a deep-fried salted-egg product and is preserved as reported with a documented note. Secondary nutrients (sodium, saturated fat, dietary fibre, sugar) estimated from HPB compositional data for comparable salted-egg fried chicken products and USDA FoodData Central reference data for fried chicken with egg coating.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on fried chicken with batter coating
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Salted Egg Chicken Chunky Pops. GI estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on analogous fried chicken with batter coating entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. The very low carbohydrate content (2.36 g/100 g) means glycaemic load per 150 g serving is approximately 1.6, which is negligible. GL = (45 × 2.36 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.59, rounded to 1.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium from salted duck egg yolk (~650 mg/100 g), exceeding the 600 mg/100 g threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Salted Egg Chicken Chunky Pops due to its very high sodium content (~650 mg/100 g from salted duck egg yolk), which substantially raises blood pressure risk; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 975 mg sodium, nearly half the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.36 g/100 g) and high protein (10.7 g/100 g) result in minimal glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Salted Egg Chicken Chunky Pops has a very low carbohydrate content of 2.36 g/100 g and high protein at 10.7 g/100 g, producing a negligible glycaemic load per serving (~1.6 GL units); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this in moderate portions but should avoid sugary dipping sauces and monitor total fat intake given the deep-fried preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation with salted egg yolk contributes saturated fat, cholesterol, and very high sodium, all of which are adverse for cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of Salted Egg Chicken Chunky Pops due to its combination of deep-fried preparation (generating saturated fat and dietary AGEs), high cholesterol from salted duck egg yolk, and very high sodium (~650 mg/100 g), all of which contribute to arterial stiffness, elevated LDL cholesterol, and raised blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; high sodium from salted egg yolk exacerbates RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Salted Egg Chicken Chunky Pops as the deep-fried preparation generates AGEs that promote joint inflammation, and the very high sodium from salted duck egg yolk worsens RA-related tissue oedema; no anti-inflammatory components are present to offset these effects.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted Egg Chicken Chunky Pops is a popular Singaporean deep-fried chicken dish in which bite-sized chicken pieces are coated in a crispy batter enriched with salted duck egg yolk sauce, typically finished with curry leaves, dried chilli, and butter. The dish is characterised by its intensely savoury, rich, and slightly creamy flavour profile derived from the salted egg yolk coating, with a crunchy exterior and juicy chicken interior. It is commonly served as a main course at lunch or dinner and is widely available at hawker centres, food courts, and casual dining restaurants across Singapore. Nutritionally, the HPB Singapore Foodo database records this dish at 55 kcal, 10.7 g protein, 0.0 g fat, and 2.36 g carbohydrate per 100 g — the reported fat value of 0.0 g is anomalous for a deep-fried product and is preserved as reported from the HPB source with a documented caveat. Sodium is estimated at approximately 650 mg/100 g due to the salted duck egg yolk, making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic load per serving is negligible (~1.6 GL units), making it relatively safe for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without sugary accompaniments. The dish contains egg, gluten, and dairy allergens and is generally considered halal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in salted egg chicken pops?
Can diabetics eat salted egg chicken?
Is salted egg chicken high in sodium?
What is salted egg chicken made of?
Is salted egg chicken suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (exact match, confidence 0.95 per input record). Confidence is set to medium rather than high because the reported fat value of 0.0 g/100 g is anomalous for a deep-fried salted-egg product and raises a data integrity concern. Secondary nutrients (sodium, sugar, dietary fibre, saturated fat) are estimated from comparable HPB entries and USDA reference data rather than directly measured for this specific product. GI is an analogous estimate.
notes: 1. fat_g is reported as 0.0 g/100 g in the HPB Singapore Foodo source (cr_id G00010399, confidence 0.95). This value is anomalous for a deep-fried salted-egg-yolk-coated chicken product, which would typically contain 8–15 g fat/100 g. The value is preserved as reported from the approved source per Project Veritas source-fidelity rules, but is flagged for re-verification against the original HPB laboratory data. Zero-macro justification: fat_g is reported as confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source entry; however, this is flagged as a likely data anomaly requiring source verification. saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0 consistent with the HPB-reported fat_g of 0.0. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profile for this specific HPB-listed salted egg chicken product is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. Sodium estimated at 650 mg/100 g based on the health_suitability reasoning in the input record, which cites this figure explicitly; this is consistent with known sodium levels in salted duck egg yolk preparations. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on fried chicken with batter coating from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found. 5. Halal status assumed based on typical commercial preparation in Singapore; not independently verified.
